Role-based and attribute-based access control for Axon (FEAT-012).\\n1\\t//!\\n2\\t//! # Layers\\n3\\t//!\\n4\\t//! **Layer 1 — RBAC** ([`Role`], [`CallerIdentity`], [`Operation`])\\n5\\t//!\\n6\\t//! Four built-in roles (`Admin \u003e Write \u003e Read \u003e None`) control access to\\n7\\t//! Axon operations.  Roles are derived from the identity provider (Tailscale\\n8\\t//! ACL tags, OIDC claims, etc.) by the server layer and passed into handlers\\n9\\t//! as a [`CallerIdentity`].  Handlers call [`CallerIdentity::check`] to\\n10\\t//! enforce the minimum required role.\\n11\\t//!\\n12\\t//! **Layer 2 — Field-level masking** ([`MaskPolicy`])\\n13\\t//!\\n14\\t//! A `MaskPolicy` hides individual entity fields from callers whose role\\n15\\t//! falls below a configured minimum.  Applied by [`CallerIdentity::apply_masks`]\\n16\\t//! before returning entity data to the caller.  Admins always see all fields.\\n17\\t//!\\n18\\t//! **Layer 3 — Collection write control** ([`WritePolicy`])\\n19\\t//!\\n20\\t//! A `WritePolicy` sets a per-collection minimum write role and marks fields\\n21\\t//! as immutable after creation.  Checked by the handler before any mutation.\\n22\\t//!\\n23\\t//! **Layer 4 — Database-scoped grants** ([`DatabaseGrant`], [`GrantRegistry`])\\n24\\t//!\\n25\\t//! Grants scope a role to a specific database (or `\\\"*\\\"` for all databases).\\n26\\t//! Without a matching grant a caller has `Role::None` on that database.\\n27\\t//! Admins with a global grant bypass per-database checks.  The grant registry\\n28\\t//! is stored in `__axon_policies__` and enforced at the routing layer.\\n29\\t\\n30\\tuse serde::{Deserialize, Serialize};\\n31\\t\\n32\\tuse crate::error::AxonError;\\n33\\t\\n34\\t/// Built-in roles for Axon RBAC (FEAT-012).\\n35\\t///\\n36\\t/// Roles are ordered by privilege: `Admin \u003e Write \u003e Read \u003e None`.\\n37\\t/// When a user has multiple roles (e.g., from multiple ACL tags),\\n38\\t/// the highest-privilege role wins.\\n39\\t#[derive(Debug, Clone, Copy, PartialEq, Eq, PartialOrd, Ord, Hash, Serialize, Deserialize)]\\n40\\tpub enum Role {\\n41\\t    /// No access. Explicitly denied.\\n42\\t    None = 0,\\n43\\t    /// Read entities, query, traverse, browse audit log.\\n44\\t    Read = 1,\\n45\\t    /// Create, update, delete entities and links in any collection.\\n46\\t    Write = 2,\\n47\\t    /// All operations including drop and schema changes.\\n48\\t    Admin = 3,\\n49\\t}\\n50\\t\\n51\\timpl Role {\\n52\\t    /// Parse a role from a Tailscale ACL tag or string name.\\n53\\t    ///\\n54\\t    /// Accepted inputs (both full `tag:` prefix and bare name):\\n55\\t    ///\\n56\\t    /// | Input | Role |\\n57\\t    /// |-------|------|\\n58\\t    /// | `\\\"tag:axon-admin\\\"` / `\\\"tag:admin\\\"` / `\\\"admin\\\"` | `Admin` |\\n59\\t    /// | `\\\"tag:axon-write\\\"` / `\\\"tag:axon-agent\\\"` / `\\\"tag:write\\\"` / `\\\"write\\\"` | `Write` |\\n60\\t    /// | `\\\"tag:axon-read\\\"` / `\\\"tag:read\\\"` / `\\\"read\\\"` | `Read` |\\n61\\t    /// | anything else | `None` |\\n62\\t    ///\\n63\\t    /// `tag:axon-agent` is an alias for `write` — the conventional tag for\\n64\\t    /// automated agent workloads that need read/write but not admin access.\\n65\\t    pub fn from_tag(tag: \u0026str) -\u003e Self {\\n66\\t        match tag {\\n67\\t            \\\"tag:axon-admin\\\" | \\\"tag:admin\\\" | \\\"admin\\\" =\u003e Role::Admin,\\n68\\t            \\\"tag:axon-write\\\" | \\\"tag:axon-agent\\\" | \\\"tag:write\\\" | \\\"write\\\" =\u003e Role::Write,\\n69\\t            \\\"tag:axon-read\\\" | \\\"tag:read\\\" | \\\"read\\\" =\u003e Role::Read,\\n70\\t            _ =\u003e Role::None,\\n71\\t        }\\n72\\t    }\\n73\\t\\n74\\t    /// Determine the highest-privilege role from a set of tags.\\n75\\t    pub fn from_tags(tags: \u0026[String]) -\u003e Self {\\n76\\t        tags.iter()\\n77\\t            .map(|t| Role::from_tag(t))\\n78\\t            .max()\\n79\\t            .unwrap_or(Role::None)\\n80\\t    }\\n81\\t}\\n82\\t\\n83\\timpl std::fmt::Display for Role {\\n84\\t    fn fmt(\u0026self, f: \u0026mut std::fmt::Formatter\u003c'_\u003e) -\u003e std::fmt::Result {\\n85\\t        match self {\\n86\\t            Role::None =\u003e write!(f, \\\"none\\\"),\\n87\\t            Role::Read =\u003e write!(f, \\\"read\\\"),\\n88\\t            Role::Write =\u003e write!(f, \\\"write\\\"),\\n89\\t            Role::Admin =\u003e write!(f, \\\"admin\\\"),\\n90\\t        }\\n91\\t    }\\n92\\t}\\n93\\t\\n94\\t/// The kind of operation being attempted.\\n95\\t#[derive(Debug, Clone, Copy, PartialEq, Eq)]\\n96\\tpub enum Operation {\\n97\\t    /// Read an entity, query, traverse, audit.\\n98\\t    Read,\\n99\\t    /// Create, update, delete entities/links.\\n100\\t    Write,\\n101\\t    /// Drop collections, modify schemas, manage namespaces.\\n102\\t    Admin,\\n103\\t}\\n104\\t\\n105\\timpl Operation {\\n106\\t    /// The minimum role required for this operation.\\n107\\t    pub fn required_role(self) -\u003e Role {\\n108\\t        match self {\\n109\\t            Operation::Read =\u003e Role::Read,\\n110\\t            Operation::Write =\u003e Role::Write,\\n111\\t            Operation::Admin =\u003e Role::Admin,\\n112\\t        }\\n113\\t    }\\n114\\t}\\n115\\t\\n116\\t/// A simple field-equality filter used as an agent scope constraint\\n117\\t/// (FEAT-022 / ADR-016).\\n118\\t///\\n119\\t/// When attached to a [`CallerIdentity`], the agent guardrails layer rejects\\n120\\t/// any mutation whose target entity data does not satisfy\\n121\\t/// `data[field] == value`. V1 supports only field-equality predicates;\\n122\\t/// compound and range filters are deferred.\\n123\\t#[derive(Debug, Clone, PartialEq, Eq, Serialize, Deserialize)]\\n124\\tpub struct EntityFilter {\\n125\\t    /// The top-level data field to compare.\\n126\\t    pub field: String,\\n127\\t    /// The required value for that field.\\n128\\t    pub value: serde_json::Value,\\n129\\t}\\n130\\t\\n131\\timpl EntityFilter {\\n132\\t    /// Convenience constructor.\\n133\\t    pub fn new(field: impl Into\u003cString\u003e, value: serde_json::Value) -\u003e Self {\\n134\\t        Self {\\n135\\t            field: field.into(),\\n136\\t            value,\\n137\\t        }\\n138\\t    }\\n139\\t\\n140\\t    /// Returns `true` when the JSON object `data` satisfies this filter.\\n141\\t    ///\\n142\\t    /// Returns `false` when `data` is not a JSON object, when the field is\\n143\\t    /// absent, or when the value does not equal [`Self::value`].\\n144\\t    pub fn matches(\u0026self, data: \u0026serde_json::Value) -\u003e bool {\\n145\\t        data.get(\u0026self.field).is_some_and(|v| v == \u0026self.value)\\n146\\t    }\\n147\\t}\\n148\\t\\n149\\t/// Identity context for a request.\\n150\\t///\\n151\\t/// Populated by the auth middleware from the identity provider.\\n152\\t/// In `--no-auth` mode, all requests get `CallerIdentity::anonymous()`.\\n153\\t#[derive(Debug, Clone, Serialize, Deserialize)]\\n154\\tpub struct CallerIdentity {\\n155\\t    /// Actor name for audit log entries.\\n156\\t    pub actor: String,\\n157\\t    /// The caller's effective role (highest privilege from all their tags).\\n158\\t    pub role: Role,\\n159\\t    /// Optional scope constraint enforced by the agent guardrails layer\\n160\\t    /// (FEAT-022, ADR-016). When `Some`, this caller may only mutate entities\\n161\\t    /// whose data matches the filter. `None` means no scope restriction.\\n162\\t    #[serde(default, skip_serializing_if = \\\"Option::is_none\\\")]\\n163\\t    pub entity_filter: Option\u003cEntityFilter\u003e,\\n164\\t}\\n165\\t\\n166\\timpl CallerIdentity {\\n167\\t    /// Create an anonymous identity with admin privileges (`--no-auth` mode).\\n168\\t    pub fn anonymous() -\u003e Self {\\n169\\t        Self {\\n170\\t            actor: \\\"anonymous\\\".into(),\\n171\\t            role: Role::Admin,\\n172\\t            entity_filter: None,\\n173\\t        }\\n174\\t    }\\n175\\t\\n176\\t    /// Create an identity from a name and role.\\n177\\t    pub fn new(actor: impl Into\u003cString\u003e, role: Role) -\u003e Self {\\n178\\t        Self {\\n179\\t            actor: actor.into(),\\n180\\t            role,\\n181\\t            entity_filter: None,\\n182\\t        }\\n183\\t    }\\n184\\t\\n185\\t    /// Attach an entity scope filter (builder style, FEAT-022).\\n186\\t    pub fn with_entity_filter(mut self, filter: EntityFilter) -\u003e Self {\\n187\\t        self.entity_filter = Some(filter);\\n188\\t        self\\n189\\t    }\\n190\\t\\n191\\t    /// Check whether this caller is authorized for the given operation.\\n192\\t    ///\\n193\\t    /// Returns `Ok(())` if authorized, or `Err(AxonError::InvalidOperation)`\\n194\\t    /// with a descriptive message if not.\\n195\\t    pub fn check(\u0026self, op: Operation) -\u003e Result\u003c(), AxonError\u003e {\\n196\\t        let required = op.required_role();\\n197\\t        if self.role \u003e= required {\\n198\\t            Ok(())\\n199\\t        } else {\\n200\\t            Err(AxonError::InvalidOperation(format!(\\n201\\t                \\\"permission denied: role '{}' cannot perform {:?} operations (requires '{}')\\\",\\n202\\t                self.role, op, required\\n203\\t            )))\\n204\\t        }\\n205\\t    }\\n206\\t\\n207\\t    /// Apply field-level masking to entity data based on mask policies.\\n208\\t    ///\\n209\\t    /// Admin users see all fields. For other roles, fields listed in a\\n210\\t    /// mask policy that requires a higher role than the caller's are\\n211\\t    /// removed from the data.\\n212\\t    pub fn apply_masks(\u0026self, data: \u0026mut serde_json::Value, policies: \u0026[MaskPolicy]) {\\n213\\t        if self.role \u003e= Role::Admin {\\n214\\t            return; // Admin sees all.\\n215\\t        }\\n216\\t        if let Some(obj) = data.as_object_mut() {\\n217\\t            for policy in policies {\\n218\\t                if self.role \u003c policy.min_role {\\n219\\t                    obj.remove(\u0026policy.field);\\n220\\t                }\\n221\\t            }\\n222\\t        }\\n223\\t    }\\n224\\t}\\n225\\t\\n226\\t/// A field-level mask policy (US-046, FEAT-012).\\n227\\t///\\n228\\t/// Specifies that a field should be hidden from callers whose role is\\n229\\t/// below `min_role`. Admin always sees all fields.\\n230\\t#[derive(Debug, Clone, PartialEq, Eq, Serialize, Deserialize)]\\n231\\tpub struct MaskPolicy {\\n232\\t    /// The field name to mask.\\n233\\t    pub field: String,\\n234\\t    /// Minimum role required to see this field.\\n235\\t    pub min_role: Role,\\n236\\t}\\n237\\t\\n238\\t// ── Attribute-based write control (US-047, FEAT-012) ────────────────────────\\n239\\t\\n240\\t/// Per-collection write policy for attribute-based access control (ABAC).\\n241\\t///\\n242\\t/// Stored in the `__axon_policies__` pseudo-collection. Defines:\\n243\\t/// - Minimum role required to write to this collection\\n244\\t/// - Fields that are immutable after creation\\n245\\t#[derive(Debug, Clone, PartialEq, Eq, Serialize, Deserialize)]\\n246\\tpub struct WritePolicy {\\n247\\t    /// Collection this policy applies to.\\n248\\t    pub collection: String,\\n249\\t    /// Minimum role required for write operations.\\n250\\t    pub min_write_role: Role,\\n251\\t    /// Fields that cannot be modified after the initial create.\\n252\\t    #[serde(default)]\\n253\\t    pub immutable_fields: Vec\u003cString\u003e,\\n254\\t}\\n255\\t\\n256\\timpl WritePolicy {\\n257\\t    /// Check if a caller has sufficient role to write to this collection.\\n258\\t    pub fn check_write(\u0026self, caller: \u0026CallerIdentity) -\u003e Result\u003c(), AxonError\u003e {\\n259\\t        if caller.role \u003e= self.min_write_role {\\n260\\t            Ok(())\\n261\\t        } else {\\n262\\t            Err(AxonError::InvalidOperation(format!(\\n263\\t                \\\"write policy for '{}': role '{}' insufficient (requires '{}')\\\",\\n264\\t                self.collection, caller.role, self.min_write_role\\n265\\t            )))\\n266\\t        }\\n267\\t    }\\n268\\t\\n269\\t    /// Check if any immutable fields have been modified.\\n270\\t    ///\\n271\\t    /// Returns the list of field names that were illegally modified.\\n272\\t    pub fn check_immutable_fields(\\n273\\t        \u0026self,\\n274\\t        old_data: \u0026serde_json::Value,\\n275\\t        new_data: \u0026serde_json::Value,\\n276\\t    ) -\u003e Vec\u003cString\u003e {\\n277\\t        let mut violations = Vec::new();\\n278\\t        for field in \u0026self.immutable_fields {\\n279\\t            let old_val = old_data.get(field);\\n280\\t            let new_val = new_data.get(field);\\n281\\t            // If the field existed before and is now different or missing, it's a violation.\\n282\\t            if old_val.is_some() \u0026\u0026 old_val != new_val {\\n283\\t                violations.push(field.clone());\\n284\\t            }\\n285\\t        }\\n286\\t        violations\\n287\\t    }\\n288\\t}\\n289\\t\\n290\\t// ── Database-scoped access control (US-038, FEAT-014) ────────────────────────\\n291\\t\\n292\\t/// A grant that scopes a role to a specific database.\\n293\\t///\\n294\\t/// Without a grant, users cannot access a database (unless they have a\\n295\\t/// wildcard `*` grant or admin role).\\n296\\t#[derive(Debug, Clone, PartialEq, Eq, JWT-based auth pipeline: sign, verify, and enforce credentials (ADR-018 §4).\\n//!\\n//! # Verification order\\n//!\\n//! 1. Extract `Authorization: Bearer \u003ctoken\u003e` → `Unauthenticated` if missing.\\n//! 2. Parse + verify HS256 signature → `CredentialInvalid` / `CredentialMalformed`.\\n//! 3. Check `iss` against issuer ID → `CredentialForeignIssuer`.\\n//! 4. Check `exp`/`nbf` with ±30 s skew → `CredentialExpired` / `CredentialNotYetValid`.\\n//! 5. Check `jti` revocation (cache → storage) → `CredentialRevoked`.\\n//! 6. Compare `aud` to URL tenant → `CredentialWrongTenant`.\\n//! 7. Look up `sub` in users table, check suspension → `UserSuspended`.\\n//! 8. Check tenant membership → `NotATenantMember`.\\n//! 9. Walk grants for URL database + HTTP-method-derived op → `DatabaseNotGranted` / `OpNotGranted`.\\n//!\\n//! On success, installs `ResolvedIdentity` into the request extension.\\n//! On failure, returns a JSON error response built from `AuthError::status_code` and\\n//! `AuthError::error_code`.\\n\\nuse std::collections::HashSet;\\nuse std::sync::{Arc, Mutex, RwLock};\\nuse std::time::{SystemTime, UNIX_EPOCH};\\n\\nuse axum::extract::State;\\nuse axum::middleware::Next;\\nuse axum::response::{IntoResponse, Response};\\nuse http::{HeaderMap, Method, StatusCode};\\nuse jsonwebtoken::{Algorithm, DecodingKey, EncodingKey, Header, Validation, decode, encode};\\nuse serde_json::json;\\nuse uuid::Uuid;\\n\\nuse axon_core::auth::{AuthError, JwtClaims, Op, ResolvedIdentity, TenantId, UserId};\\nuse axon_storage::adapter::StorageAdapter;\\n\\n// ── JwtIssuer ────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────\\n\\n/// Signs and verifies JWTs using HS256.\\npub struct JwtIssuer {\\n    secret: Vec\u003cu8\u003e,\\n    /// Issuer identifier placed in the `iss` claim and verified on every request.\\n    pub issuer_id: String,\\n}\\n\\nimpl JwtIssuer {\\n    /// Create a new issuer with the given HMAC secret and issuer ID.\\n    pub fn new(secret: Vec\u003cu8\u003e, issuer_id: String) -\u003e Self {\\n        Self { secret, issuer_id In `Tailscale` mode the server contacts the local Tailscale daemon over its\\n//! Clock-skew boundary at ±30 s\\n//! - HTTP-method × grant-ops matrix\\n\\nuse std::sync::{Arc, Mutex};\\nuse std::time::{SystemTime, UNIX_EPOCH};\\n\\nuse axum::body::Body;\\nuse axum::extract::Extension;\\nuse axum::http::StatusCode;\\nuse axum::response::IntoResponse;\\nuse axum::routing::get;\\nuse axum::Router;\\nuse http::Request;\\nuse serde::Serialize;\\nuse serde_json::Value;\\nuse tower::ServiceExt;\\nuse uuid::Uuid;\\n\\nuse axon_core::auth::{\\n    AuthError, GrantedDatabase, Grants, JwtClaims, Op, ResolvedIdentity, TenantId, TenantMember,\\n    TenantRole, User, UserId,\\n};\\nuse axon_server::auth_pipeline::{\\n    AuthErrorResponse, AuthPipelineState, InMemoryRevocationCache, JwtIssuer,\\n    jwt_verify_layer,\\n};\\nuse axon_storage::MemoryStorageAdapter;\\n\\n// ── Test helpers ──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────\\n\\nconst ISSUER_ID: \u0026str = \\\"test-issuer\\\";\\nconst TENANT: \u0026str = \\\"acme\\\";\\nconst DATABASE: \u0026str = \\\"orders\\\";\\nconst USER_ID: \u0026str = \\\"user-01\\\";\\nconst SECRET: \u0026[u8] = b\\\"super-secret-key-for-tests\\\";\\nconst TEST_PATH: \u0026str = \\\"/tenants/acme/databases/orders/ping\\\";\\n\\nfn now_secs() -\u003e u64 {\\n    SystemTime::now()\\n        .duration_since(UNIX_EPOCH)\\n        .unwrap_or_default()\\n        .as_secs()\\n}\\n\\nfn valid_claims() -\u003e JwtClaims {\\n    let now = now_secs();\\n    JwtClaims {\\n        iss: ISSUER_ID.to_string(),\\n        sub: USER_ID.to_string(),\\n        aud: TENANT.to_string(),\\n        jti: Uuid::now_v7().to_string(),\\n        iat: now,\\n        nbf: now,\\n        exp: now + 3600,\\n        grants: Grants {\\n            databases: vec![GrantedDatabase {\\n                name: DATABASE.to_string(),\\n                ops: vec![Op::Read, Op::Write],\\n            }],\\n        },\\n    }\\n}\\n\\nfn make_storage() -\u003e MemoryStorageAdapter {\\n    let mut storage = MemoryStorageAdapter::default();\\n    storage.insert_user(User {\\n        id: UserId::new(USER_ID),\\n        display_name: \\\"Test User\\\".to_string(),\\n        email: None,\\n        created_at_ms: 0,\\n        suspended_at_ms: None,\\n    });\\n    storage.insert_tenant_member(TenantMember {\\n        tenant_id: TenantId::new(TENANT),\\n        user_id: UserId::new(USER_ID),\\n        role: TenantRole::Write,\\n    });\\n    storage\\n}\\n\\nfn make_state() -\u003e Arc\u003cAuthPipelineState\u003e {\\n    let issuer = JwtIssuer::new(SECRET.to_vec(), ISSUER_ID.to_string());\\n    let storage: Box\u003cdyn axon_storage::StorageAdapter + Send + Sync\u003e =\\n        Box::new(make_storage());\\n    Arc::new(AuthPipelineState {\\n        issuer: Arc::new(issuer),\\n        revocation_cache: Arc::new(InMemoryRevocationCache::new()),\\n        storage: Arc::new(Mutex::new(storage)),\\n    })\\n}\\n\\nfn make_app(state: Arc\u003cAuthPipelineState\u003e) -\u003e Router {\\n    Router::new()\\n        .route(TEST_PATH, get(|| async { StatusCode::OK }))\\n        .layer(axum::middleware::from_fn_with_state(\\n            state,\\n            jwt_verify_layer,\\n        ))\\n}\\n\\nasync fn send(app: Router, req: Request\u003cBody\u003e) -\u003e (StatusCode, Value) {\\n    let resp = app.oneshot(req).await.expect(\\\"service call should succeed\\\");\\n    let status = resp.status();\\n    let bytes = axum::body::to_bytes(resp.into_body(), 64 * 1024)\\n        .await\\n        .expect(\\\"body read should succeed\\\");\\n    let json: Value = serde_json::from_slice(\u0026bytes).unwrap_or(Value::Null);\\n    (status, json)\\n}\\n\\nfn get_req(path: \u0026str) -\u003e Request\u003cBody\u003e {\\n    Request::builder()\\n        .method(\\\"GET\\\")\\n        .uri(path)\\n        .body(Body::empty())\\n        .unwrap()\\n}\\n\\nfn bearer_req(path: \u0026str, token: \u0026str) -\u003e Request\u003cBody\u003e {\\n    Request::builder()\\n        .method(\\\"GET\\\")\\n        .uri(path)\\n        .header(\\\"Authorization\\\", format!(\\\"Bearer {token}\\\"))\\n        .body(Body::empty())\\n        .unwrap()\\n}\\n\\nfn error_code(json: \u0026Value) -\u003e \u0026str {\\n    json[\\\"error\\\"][\\\"code\\\"].as_str().unwrap_or(\\\"\\\")\\n}\\n\\n// ── JWT round-trip ────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────\\n\\n#[test]\\nfn jwt_roundtrip_succeeds() {\\n    let issuer = JwtIssuer::new(SECRET.to_vec(), ISSUER_ID.to_string());\\n    let claims = valid_claims();\\n    let token = issuer.issue(\u0026claims).expect(\\\"issue should succeed\\\");\\n    let decoded = issuer.verify(\u0026token).expect(\\\"verify should succeed\\\");\\n    assert_eq!(decoded.iss, claims.iss);\\n    assert_eq!(decoded.sub, claims.sub);\\n    assert_eq!(decoded.aud, claims.aud);\\n    assert_eq!(decoded.jti, claims.jti);\\n    assert_eq!(decoded.grants, claims.grants);\\n}\\n\\n// ── ADR-018 §4 failure-mode table ─────────────────────────────────────────────\\n\\n/// Row 1: No Authorization header → 401 unauthenticated\\n#[tokio::test]\\nasync fn error_no_auth_header() {\\n    let state = make_state();\\n    let app = make_app(state);\\n    let req = get_req(TEST_PATH);\\n    let (status, json) = send(app, req).await;\\n    assert_eq!(status, StatusCode::UNAUTHORIZED);\\n jwt_verify_layer};\\nuse axon_storage::MemoryStorageAdapter;\\n\\nconst ISSUER: \u0026str = \\\"integration-issuer\\\";\\nconst TENANT: \u0026str = \\\"acme\\\";\\nconst DATABASE: \u0026str = \\\"orders\\\";\\nconst USER_ID: \u0026str = \\\"u-integration-01\\\";\\nconst SECRET: \u0026[u8] = b\\\"integration-test-secret\\\";\\nconst PROTECTED_PATH: \u0026str = .as_secs()\\n}\\n\\nfn make_state() -\u003e Arc\u003cAuthPipelineState\u003e {\\n    let mut storage = MemoryStorageAdapter::default();\\n    storage.insert_user(User {\\n        id: storage.insert_tenant_member(TenantMember {\\n        tenant_id: TenantId::new(TENANT),\\n        user_id: UserId::new(USER_ID),\\n        role: TenantRole::Write,\\n    });\\n    Arc::new(AuthPipelineState {\\n        issuer: Arc::new(JwtIssuer::new(SECRET.to_vec(), ISSUER.to_string())),\\n        revocation_cache: Arc::new(InMemoryRevocationCache::new()),\\n        storage: Arc::new(Mutex::new(\\n            Box::new(storage) as Box\u003cdyn axon_storage::StorageAdapter + Send + Sync\u003e\\n        )),\\n    })\\n}\\n\\nfn make_app(state: Arc\u003cAuthPipelineState\u003e) -\u003e Router {\\n    Router::new()\\n        .route(PROTECTED_PATH, get(|| async { StatusCode::OK }))\\n        .layer(axum::middleware::from_fn_with_state(\\n format!(\\\"Bearer {token}\\\")\\n}\\n\\nfn valid_token(issuer: \u0026JwtIssuer, grants: Grants) -\u003e String {\\n    let now = now_secs();\\n    let claims = JwtClaims {\\n        iss: 3600,\\n        grants,\\n    };\\n    issuer.issue(\u0026claims).expect(\\\"issue should succeed\\\")\\n}\\n\\nfn orders_read_grants() -\u003e Grants {\\n    Grants {\\n        databases: directory for this skill: /home/erik/.claude/skills/helix-commit\\n\\n# Commit: HELIX-Compliant Git Commit\\n\\nCreate a well-formatted commit with issue traceability, run the project's\\nbuild gate, push, and optionally update the tracker.\\n\\n## When to Use\\n\\n- After implementing a tracked issue\\n- When you need a properly formatted commit message\\n- When codex or another agent needs commit support\\n\\n## What It Does\\n\\n1. **Inspect** staged changes (`git diff --cached --stat`)\\n2. **Format** commit message:\\n   - First line: `\u003cissue-id\u003e \u003cconcise summary\u003e` (under 72 chars)\\n   - Body: what changed and why, governing artifact refs\\n   - Trailer: verification commands run\\n3. **Pre-push gate**: run the project's build check before pushing\\n   - `lefthook run pre-commit` if available\\n   - `cargo check --workspace` for Rust projects\\n   - `npm test` for Node.js projects\\n4. **Commit and push**: `git commit`, `git pull --rebase`, `git push`\\n5. **Tracker update** (if issue-id provided): close the issue\\n\\n## Commit Message Format\\n\\n```\\nhx-abc123 Short summary of the change\\n\\nLonger description of what changed and why. Reference governing\\nartifacts when relevant (SD-001, TP-002, etc.).\\n\\nVerification: cargo test -p changed-crate, cargo clippy\\n```\\n\\n## Examples\\n\\n```bash\\nhelix commit hx-abc123      # commit, gate, push, close issue\\nhelix commit                 # commit and gate without tracker update\\n```\\n\"}]},\"parent_tool_use_id\":null,\"session_id\":\"80f0eb9d-4064-4d06-8560-e247ce1170f2\",\"uuid\":\"389e7b5b-4f0d-4836-a072-b1bc977baa31\",\"timestamp\":\"2026-04-15T01:49:25.805Z\",\"isSynthetic\":true}\n{\"type\":\"assistant\",\"message\":{\"model\":\"claude-sonnet-4-6\",\"id\":\"msg_01P2d4aV4hoZu9dRSEoGCXwm\",\"type\":\"message\",\"role\":\"assistant\",\"content\":[{\"type\":\"thinking\",\"thinking\":\"Now I'll perform the helix commit following the skill instructions. cli/internal/agent/testdata/reviewer/bead-jsonl-oversized-line.jsonl:1:{"acceptance":"- [ ] JWT middleware installed in data-plane router as optional layer (runs when Authorization header present)\n- [ ] JwtIssuer constructed at startup in serve.rs (dev: random key with warning)\n- [ ] AuditAttribution populated on every audit write when ResolvedIdentity is present\n- [ ] gRPC service.rs migrated from x-axon-database to composite x-axon-tenant-database\n- [ ] gRPC service.rs gained optional JWT verify path\n- [ ] cutover_jwt_test.rs integration test with real JWT end-to-end\n- [ ] scripts/cutover-smoke.sh script runs end-to-end against dev server\n- [ ] cargo test --workspace green\n- [ ] cargo clippy --workspace -- -D warnings clean\n- [ ] Legacy auth paths (--no-auth, --guest-role, Tailscale) still work unchanged","closing_commit_sha":"1a378fe785612ab9a24241977cf60c0c13e150b9","created_at":"2026-04-15T11:54:30.489788638Z","description":"Finish wiring the ADR-018 auth stack into the real request pipeline. After the smoke test on commit e83cf02, D3 already landed more than expected: `resolve_tenant_handler` composes `{tenant}:{database}` into the TenantRouter slug, giving real per-pair physical isolation. What remains is the three things that are *built but not installed*.\n\n## What is already live (confirmed by smoke test)\n\n- Path-based routing: /tenants/:t/databases/:d/... routed to per-pair SQLite files via composite slug\n- GraphQL under the nested path\n- Unprefixed REST + /graphql return 404\n- HttpAxonClient in the SDK + the UI use the new paths\n- Legacy X-Axon-Database header is gone from REST\n\n## What is still NOT installed (the gap this bead closes)\n\n### 1. JWT pipeline not enforced\n\nB1 landed `auth_pipeline::jwt_verify_layer` + `JwtIssuer`. The real gateway router in `crates/axon-server/src/gateway.rs` does NOT install this layer. The server currently runs in legacy auth mode (Tailscale or --no-auth). Install the layer as an OPTIONAL middleware: if the request has an Authorization header, run the JWT pipeline; if not, fall back to legacy auth.\n\nWire point: in `build_router_with_auth` (or wherever the data-plane nested router is constructed), add `.layer(from_fn_with_state(state.clone(), jwt_verify_layer))` before the tenant_handler middleware. The layer needs access to a `JwtIssuer` and the `StorageAdapter`. Add them to the AppState struct.\n\nCreate the JwtIssuer at server startup in `serve.rs`. Read the signing key from an env var `AXON_JWT_KEY` (base64 HMAC secret); if unset, generate a random 32-byte key at startup and log a warning (dev mode). Production key management is a future concern.\n\nThe legacy auth path (crates/axon-server/src/auth.rs Identity + Role) still works for requests without an Authorization header — the JWT layer is a no-op pass-through when the header is absent. This preserves --no-auth, --guest-role, and Tailscale whois behavior exactly as today.\n\n### 2. Audit attribution not populated\n\nF1 added `AuditAttribution` as an optional field on `AuditEntry` plus the `with_attribution()` builder. No handler currently calls it. In the JWT middleware (step 1), after `ResolvedIdentity` is installed into the request extension, also expose a `current_attribution()` helper on AppState that the audit write sites can call.\n\nFind every `AuditEntry::new(...)` call site in `crates/axon-server/src/gateway.rs` (there are probably 10-20) and chain `.with_attribution(current_attribution(\u0026request))` when a ResolvedIdentity is present. When absent (legacy auth), leave the attribution as None — the existing `actor: String` field still works.\n\n### 3. gRPC service.rs still reads x-axon-database\n\n`crates/axon-server/src/service.rs:26` has `const AXON_DATABASE_HEADER: \u0026str = \"x-axon-database\"` and `grpc_current_database` reads it from metadata. The gRPC service is entirely separate from the REST gateway and D3 did not touch it. Replace this header read with path-based extraction... except gRPC doesn't have URL paths in the same way. gRPC calls use service + method names, not paths. The database has to come from either:\n\n(a) A metadata field (still a header-equivalent), OR\n(b) A new field on every request proto\n\n(a) is pragmatic. Rename the metadata header from `x-axon-database` to `x-axon-tenant-database` and carry a composite value `\"{tenant}:{database}\"`. This matches the REST composite slug. The gRPC service parses it and dispatches via the same `get_or_create_any` path.\n\nAlso add `x-axon-authorization` (or reuse the standard `authorization` metadata key) for JWT credentials. Run the JWT verify pipeline in the gRPC `check_auth` step before dispatching to the handler.\n\nFor this bead, the gRPC change is small:\n- Rename the metadata constant\n- Update `grpc_current_database` to parse the composite form\n- Update the Rust + TS gRPC clients to send the composite form\n- Add JWT auth path in `check_auth`\n\n### 4. DatabaseRouter NOT swapped in (deferred — not needed right now)\n\nD2 landed `DatabaseRouter` as a new primitive, but the gateway still uses `TenantRouter::get_or_create_any(slug)`. The smoke test confirmed this composite slug works correctly. Replacing TenantRouter with DatabaseRouter is a refactor that doesn't change behavior — defer it to a later cleanup bead. Not in this bead's scope.\n\n## Scope summary\n\nThis bead does exactly three things:\n\n1. Install B1's JWT middleware in the data-plane router, optional path (Authorization header present)\n2. Populate F1's AuditAttribution at every audit write site when ResolvedIdentity is present\n3. Migrate gRPC service.rs from x-axon-database header to the composite slug + optional JWT metadata\n\n## Tests for this bead\n\n### Integration test: crates/axon-server/tests/cutover_jwt_test.rs (NEW)\n\n- create a JwtIssuer with a known key\n- mint a JWT for a default user with grants on (default, default)\n- start the in-process test server harness that gateway_test.rs uses\n- send a request with Authorization: Bearer \u003cjwt\u003e to /tenants/default/databases/default/collections\n- assert 200 (the JWT pipeline accepted + the handler saw ResolvedIdentity)\n\n### Integration test: attribution populated\n\n- send an entity-create request with a JWT\n- query the audit log\n- assert the audit entry has `attribution.user_id` set to the JWT sub\n\n### gRPC test: composite slug\n\n- send a CreateEntity gRPC request with metadata `x-axon-tenant-database: acme:orders`\n- assert routing to the acme:orders database (check the storage file exists)\n\n### Manual smoke test\n\nStart the server, issue a JWT via `/control/tenants/default/credentials`, use the HttpAxonClient with the JWT, create an entity, verify the audit log has attribution. Write a short shell script under `scripts/cutover-smoke.sh` that does this end-to-end.\n\n## Out of scope\n\n- DatabaseRouter replacement — deferred\n- OIDC federation — follow-up\n- Signing key rotation — future ADR\n- Removing legacy Identity/Role types from auth.rs — they still serve the --no-auth and Tailscale paths\n\n## Files to touch\n\n- crates/axon-server/src/gateway.rs — install JWT middleware, thread AuditAttribution into audit writes (10-20 sites)\n- crates/axon-server/src/serve.rs — construct JwtIssuer at startup\n- crates/axon-server/src/service.rs — rename header constant, parse composite, add JWT path\n- crates/axon-server/tests/cutover_jwt_test.rs — NEW\n- scripts/cutover-smoke.sh — NEW\n- Possibly sdk/typescript/src/client.ts — update gRPC metadata key if touching gRPC SDK surface\n\n## Files NOT to touch\n\n- crates/axon-server/src/auth_pipeline.rs — B1 is canonical, just consume it\n- crates/axon-server/src/auth.rs — legacy Tailscale path, out of scope for removal\n- crates/axon-storage/* — no storage changes\n- crates/axon-core/* — no type changes\n\n## Verification\n\n- cargo test -p axon-server (all prior tests green + new cutover_jwt_test)\n- cargo clippy --workspace -- -D warnings clean\n- scripts/cutover-smoke.sh runs end-to-end against a dev   id: API-001
  depends_on:
    - FEAT-004

# API/Interface Contract: Execute-Bead Supervisor

## Purpose

This contract defines the first shipped supervision loop for `ddx try` style
bead-attempt work. It is intentionally single-project scoped and consumes DDx's
generic execution readiness and execution-result surfaces rather than any
HELIX-specific hidden policy.

The supervisor contract covers:

- one project context at a time
- one ordinary executable bead at a time
- bead claim and selection flow
- structural execution validation
- delegated execute-bead execution
- observation of execute-bead result states
- operator controls and loop observability

## Boundary

DDx owns the generic substrate:

- bead storage and claim state
- execution-definition validation and run recording
- git worktree mechanics
- hidden-ref preservation of non-landed iterations

HELIX owns the workflow policy:

- which beads are eligible for the loop
- when the loop should run
- retry policy after a failure
- what operator-facing automation wraps the loop

The first shipped supervisor is intentionally **not** the epic worker. Epic
execution uses a separate worker mode because it needs a persistent branch,
worktree, and merge-commit landing contract.

`ddx try` remains the single owner of required execution document resolution,
required post-run checks, merge-eligibility evaluation, and land/preserve
mechanics, including creation and cleanup of its isolated worktree. The
supervisor only orchestrates queue selection, invokes that command from the
project root, and records the result states emitted by that
command.

The command requires a reproducible git base revision, not a pristine root
checkout. Tracked root changes may be checkpointed into an immutable base
commit before launch; ignored runtime scratch must not block the attempt.

The first release does not require multi-project discovery, cross-project
scheduling, or multi-host coordination. A worker instance binds to exactly one
project context for the duration of a loop.

Each attempt also has a tracked execution-evidence bundle under
`.ddx/executions/<attempt-id>/`. The supervisor may read and surface those
artifacts, but it does not reinterpret them as generic `exec-runs`
attachments; the bundle is the canonical tracked evidence for one
`execute-bead` attempt.

The bundle contents and write order are owned by `execute-bead` and
specified in FEAT-006 §"Execute-Bead Evidence Bundle". At minimum, the
bundle contains `prompt.md`, `manifest.json`, and `result.json`, and is
committed alongside the iteration (landed or preserved). The supervisor
must not treat the absence of any of those files as a normal post-run
state.

The prompt delivered to the agent for each attempt is compiled by the
**execute-bead prompt rationalizer** from bead fields and resolved governing
references (see FEAT-006 §"Prompt Rationalizer Contract"). The rationalizer
writes `prompt.md` to the bundle before the agent runs; this file is the
authoritative record of exactly what the agent received. The supervisor does
not author or modify the prompt.

Each iteration commit (landed or preserved under a hidden ref) carries the
canonical Git trailer set defined in FEAT-006 §"Canonical Git trailers":
`Ddx-Attempt-Id`, `Ddx-Worker-Id`, `Ddx-Harness`, `Ddx-Model`, and
`Ddx-Result-Status`. The supervisor relies on `Ddx-Attempt-Id` and
`Ddx-Result-Status` when projecting its observability surface from commit
history, and treats the trailer set as authoritative alongside the
supervisor-visible `status` field emitted by `execute-bead`.

## Single-Project State Machine

The supervision loop is a bounded state machine that repeats until the queue is
drained, the operator stops it, or a fatal project error occurs.

1. Resolve one project context from the current repository, explicit selector,
   or `ddx server` project binding.
2. Resolve the effective base revision and the governing execution contract
   snapshot that will govern this iteration.
3. Read the ready bead set for that project and order candidates by the
   supervisor's queue policy for that project context.
   - Ready non-epic beads are ordered ahead of ready epics at the same
     priority.
   - Open epics are excluded from this worker's launch set by default.
4. Run the generic execution-ready validator against the ordered candidate set
   to filter structural ineligible beads against the resolved base snapshot.
5. Claim the first validated bead atomically.
6. Run `ddx try` against the bead from the project root and
   capture its documented result schema.
7. Classify the outcome reported by `execute-bead` from the documented
   supervisor-visible `status` field using the canonical taxonomy below.
8. Continue scanning the same project queue.

The loop must never infer readiness from HELIX-specific hidden policy. It uses
the explicit queue ordering policy, the shared validator output, and the
documented result schema as its source of truth.

## Epic Boundary

Epics are first-class tracker items, but they are not consumed by the ordinary
single-ticket supervisor.

- The single-ticket supervisor drains executable child beads and other
  non-epic work.
- A separate epic-scoped worker owns one persistent epic branch and worktree.
- That worker executes child beads sequentially inside the epic worktree,
  commits each child result on the epic branch, and merges the completed epic
  to the target branch with a regular merge commit.

The epic worker's branch, worktree, child-execution, merge-gate, and final
merge-commit contract is defined in API-002 (Epic Worker Surfaces) and the
"Epic Execution Workflow" section of FEAT-006. The single-ticket supervisor
must observe these boundary rules even though it does not operate on epics
itself:

1. **No epic claims.** This supervisor must never claim an epic bead. Ready
   epics are filtered out of its candidate set even when they pass the
   structural execution validator.
2. **Child beads during an active epic.** When a child bead belongs to an
   epic that is currently being executed by an epic worker, this supervisor
   must not claim that child. The epic worker is the authoritative consumer
   of in-flight epic children. The supervisor treats such children as
   already claimed by the epic worker.
3. **Child beads for idle epics.** Children of epics that have no active
   epic worker remain ineligible for this supervisor by default. Promoting
   an orphaned child to the single-ticket queue is a workflow-tool decision,
   not a supervisor decision.
4. **Child close semantics.** When the epic worker closes a child mid-epic,
   the child's result commit lives on the epic branch (`ddx/epics/<epic-id>`)
   and has not yet reached the target branch. This supervisor must treat
   such a child as "closed on epic" rather than "landed on target" when
   computing readiness for its own queue — downstream beads that depend on a
   child closed on epic are not yet eligible for single-ticket execution on
   the target branch until the epic itself merges.
5. **Final landing is not this supervisor's job.** Landing the completed
   epic with a `--no-ff` merge commit is owned by the epic worker. This
   supervisor does not evaluate epic merge gates, does not rebase the epic
   branch, and does not perform the final merge.

This contract therefore remains the single-ticket worker contract even after
epic execution is introduced elsewhere in the system.

## Execute-Bead Result Schema

The supervisor consumes only the documented result envelope emitted by
`ddx try`:

- `status`: canonical supervisor-visible status, delegated to the
  `ExecuteBeadStatus*` enum in `cli/internal/agent/execute_bead_status.go`
- `detail`: optional operator-facing text for logging and diagnostics

The supervisor must not infer state from free-form reason strings. It uses the
`status` field for control flow and may surface `detail` for observability.
FEAT-010 defines the broader layer-2 / layer-3 lifecycle, and API-001 only
publishes the supervisor-visible projection of that implementation enum.

| Status | Bucket | Supervisor-facing disposition |
|---|---|---|
| `success` | close | Close the bead and count the attempt as a success. |
| `already_satisfied` | close | Close the bead as already satisfied; this is the success-adjacent terminal close path. |
| `no_changes` | close-or-unclaim | This is the only conditional row: a verified already-satisfied rationale may close the bead, otherwise the loop keeps it open for retry or inspection. |
| `structural_validation_failed` | unclaim | Validation failed before launch; leave the bead open. |
| `execution_failed` | unclaim | Preserve the attempt evidence and leave the bead open. |
| `post_run_check_failed` | unclaim | Preserve the attempt evidence and leave the bead open. |
| `ratchet_failed` | unclaim | Preserve the attempt evidence and leave the bead open. |
| `land_conflict` | unclaim | Preserve the attempt evidence and leave the bead open until conflict recovery or operator action. |
| `push_failed` | unclaim | Preserve the attempt evidence and leave the bead open; the push side of landing failed. |
| `push_conflict` | unclaim | Preserve the attempt evidence, park the bead, and leave it open for retry or recovery. |
| `preserved_needs_review` | operator-attention | Park the bead for operator review. |
| `no_evidence_produced` | unclaim | Leave the bead open; the attempt produced no durable evidence. |
| `resource_exhausted` | operator-attention | Stop the worker loop and surface host-resource exhaustion for operator action. |
| `declined_needs_decomposition` | operator-attention | Park the bead for decomposition rather than retrying the current shape. |
| `review_request_changes` | unclaim | Reopen the bead for repair or a follow-up attempt. |
| `review_block` | unclaim | Reopen the bead for repair or a follow-up attempt. |
| `review_malfunction` | operator-attention | Park the bead because the review path malfunctioned. |
| `review_request_clarification` | operator-attention | Park the bead for operator clarification. |
| `land_conflict_unresolvable` | operator-attention | Park the bead because automated conflict recovery failed. |
| `land_conflict_operator_required` | operator-attention | Move the bead to the operator lane. |
| `review_terminal_block` | operator-attention | Move the bead to the operator lane because the review verdict is terminal. |
| `review_fixable_gap` | unclaim | Reopen the bead for a repair cycle. |
| `repair-cycle-exhausted` | operator-attention | Park the bead after the repair cycle budget is exhausted. |

There are no future-only statuses in the current contract. The earlier
five-status API-001 draft was a historical minimal subset and is superseded by
the taxonomy above. `repair-cycle-exhausted` keeps its historical hyphenated
wire spelling for compatibility with existing evidence.

`execute-bead` result classification is based on the managed worktree outcome,
not solely on agent-authored commits. If the agent leaves tracked file edits
without creating commits, `execute-bead` synthesizes the result commit and
then evaluates land/preserve semantics. Only a truly clean managed worktree may
be reported as `no_changes`.

## Validation And Retry Semantics

Structural validation happens before any irreversible execution step.

- If a bead fails structural validation, the supervisor records the blocker,
  unclaims the bead, and leaves it open for later correction.
- If execution starts and later fails, `execute-bead` preserves the iteration
  under a hidden ref using the documented naming scheme, and the supervisor
  records that result `status`.
- If post-run required checks fail, `execute-bead` preserves the iteration
  under a hidden ref, sets the documented failure `status`, and the supervisor
  records that result `status`.
- If landing fails after a successful run, `execute-bead` preserves the
  iteration and the preserved iteration remains the canonical evidence for
  that attempt.

Retry surface:

- operators or workflow tooling fix the governing docs, bead content, or
  environment
- the bead is unclaimed and made ready again
- the next loop pass may claim it and create a new iteration

Previous preserved refs remain immutable. A retry does not rewrite or reuse the
prior attempt.

## Land And Preserve Semantics

> **Scope note:** These semantics describe the multi-machine coordinator
> topology (SD-020). For single-node mode (the FEAT-010 default), `Land()` is
> local-only; the fetch+push lifecycle described below applies only when
> multi-machine coordination is active.

Success is only complete after the result is landed through the mode-appropriate
history-preserving flow.

- execute-bead keeps landing local: when the target is unchanged, it updates
  the target with `update-ref`; when the target has advanced, it records a
  `--no-ff` merge commit so the execution branch is not rewritten
- future multi-machine coordination, if enabled elsewhere, is governed by
  SD-020; this supervisor observes landed or preserved state rather than
  performing remote coordination itself
- preserve the iteration under a hidden ref when the result cannot be landed or

# Execute-Bead Self-Commit Contract

`ddx try` runs an agent inside an isolated worktree. The agent must produce
exactly one of two durable signals before it exits:

- a git commit containing the completed implementation and tests
- `.ddx/executions/<attempt-id>/no_changes_rationale.txt`

The rationale file is only for attempts that cannot produce a valid commit. It
must explain what is done, what is blocking, and what a follow-up attempt needs.
DDx treats that as a legitimate `no_changes` signal.

If the agent exits without a commit and without the rationale file, DDx records
`no_evidence_produced`. That status is a harness failure, not success and not
ordinary no-changes. DDx does not land an execution-evidence-only iteration, and
the reviewer does not need to spend a pass discovering that the diff contains
only `.ddx/executions` metadata.

When the worktree still has uncommitted paths at that point, DDx includes those
paths in `result.json` as `no_evidence_paths` so an operator can diagnose
whether the agent edited ignored files, failed to commit, or wrote outside the
intended scope.

If it does, the fix belongs at the success classifier or close gate, not in the generic gate evaluator.

data.text: I found a second verification surface: `PostLandCommand` runs after the target branch advances, which means success can still be revoked if the exact command fails in the landed tree. I’m checking whether that already covers the failure shape or whether the missing piece is wiring the bead’s own acceptance command into that gate and proving it with a focused regression test.

The landing code already has the enforcement hook, so I’m treating this as a regression-hardening pass: add a test that models the real failure shape with a successful worker result, a post-land command that checks for a file the diff never delivered, and preservation instead of close. After that I’ll run the package tests and the repo gates.

I hit a bad workdir typo while reading the land test file. I’m reopening it from the current execution worktree and then I’ll patch the regression in the narrowest test file that already owns the land gate behavior.

data.text: I’m adding a focused regression in the landing tests: a `task_succeeded` result whose post-land command checks for files that the commit never delivered. That reproduces the shape of “the bead’s own test gate fails against its own diff” and proves the result is preserved instead of closed.
The regression is in place. I’m formatting that file, then I’ll run the agent package tests around the landing path before widening to the full `cd cli && go test ./...` gate.
The regression test is running now. If it passes, I’ll widen to the full `cli` module test suite; if it fails, I’ll adjust before moving on so I don’t burn time on a broken baseline.

The targeted test run is still compiling or executing without output. I’m letting it finish rather than interrupting, because the repo’s agent package is large and a premature cancel would give us a false failure signal.

The bead's <description> and <acceptance> are the contract: every AC must be provably satisfied by a specific code, test, or file after your commit.

## Step 0: size check

Too big if any holds:

- More than ~6 ACs spanning unrelated subsystems.
- AC mixes design, implementation, integration tests, and docs.
- Description names multiple feature-sized pieces.
- More than ~500 lines across ~5+ files in unrelated packages.
- If the bead description exceeds 8000 bytes, use a split-first pass.
- Auto-decomposition is capped at depth 2: root beads may split once, children once more; reject third-level splits with an explanation.

If too big, decompose:

1. `ddx bead create` for each child (copy parent's labels and spec-id).
2. `ddx bead dep add` only for legitimate child-to-child or sibling/replacement edges; never the parent.
3. Use `parent=<parent-id>` metadata and wire `parent -> child`.
4. Write `no_changes_rationale.txt` under the bead metadata `bundle` path with child IDs, then stop.

Decomposition alone is success. Don't mix it with implementation.

## How to work

- Read first. If the bead names files, specs, or prior beads, read them before editing — do not guess.
- Cross-reference each AC to concrete evidence (test, file, function) before committing. If you cannot point at it, it is not done.
- Run the project's test and lint commands before committing. **Do not commit red code** — fix failures first.
- Run git/index mutations sequentially; don't parallelize `git add`, `git commit`, or staging/commit commands.
- Stage with `git add <specific-paths>`; never `git add -A` (the worktree may have unrelated WIP).
- If `lefthook run pre-commit` depends on staged files, rerun it after staging the exact commit set. A `no-staged-files` run is not acceptance evidence.
- Commit exactly once when green; subject ends with `[<bead-id>]`. Stop after the commit.
- Do not modify files outside the bead's scope.
- Current-bead lifecycle is orchestrator-owned. Do not run `ddx bead update <bead-id> --claim`, `ddx bead update <bead-id> --status <status>`, `ddx bead update <bead-id> --unclaim`, or `ddx bead close <bead-id>`. Step 0 allows `ddx bead create`, `ddx bead dep add` for child-to-child or sibling/replacement edges, and `ddx bead update <parent-id> --notes 'decomposed into <child-ids>'`.
- If you cannot finish, write `no_changes_rationale.txt` under the bead metadata `bundle` path before exiting. No commit or rationale ⇒ DDx records `no_evidence_produced`.

## no_changes contract

The `no_changes_rationale.txt` file under the bead metadata `bundle` path must contain one of:

- `verification_command: <cmd>` — repo cwd; exit 0 closes, nonzero rejects.
- `status: open` + `reason: <retryable>` — open, smart retry.
- `status: proposed` + `reason: <operator needed>` — operator lane.
- `status: blocked` + `reason: <external blocker>` — blocked lane.

No pseudo-statuses. Bare rationales and `status: needs_investigation` are rejected.

For orchestrator decomposition, add `orchestrator_action: decompose` alongside `status: open`.

## Reports

Reports go under the bead metadata `bundle` path in `.ddx/executions/`. **Never write reports to `/tmp` or outside the repo**. Use a named in-repo path when given; otherwise write `<short-name>.md` there. Commit it with the code.

## Mode and overrides

DDX_MODE=bead_execution: edit code/docs for bead AC. Only broad queue-steward default is overridden; tracker, merge-policy, verification, safety stay active. Bead description/AC override CLAUDE.md, AGENTS.md, and defaults.

## Review gate

- The review is a gate, not an escape hatch — meet every AC in this pass.
- Address every BLOCKING `<review-findings>` item; do not declare `no_changes` with blocking findings open.

## Constraints

- Work only inside this execution worktree.
- Keep `.ddx/executions/` intact — DDx uses it as execution evidence.
- **Never run `ddx init`** — the workspace is initialized.
- Do not modify files outside the bead's named scope.
- Do not rewrite CLAUDE.md, AGENTS.md, or other instruction files unless the bead asks.

## Long-running matrix/benchmark beads

For expensive commands (benchmarks, load tests, validation > 60s per variant):

- Write a matrix plan before launching expensive commands: list required configs, output paths, completion criteria.

Parent bead ddx-53df5e2f is too broad for one execution pass. This child owns part 1 of 2 of that parent scope.

ROOT CAUSE
# PROBLEM
The legacy `*Store.MigrateToAxon` method (now exposed via `Migrator.MigrateToAxon` in MIG bead, returning `ErrDeprecated`) writes JSONL files under `.ddx/axon/` rather than performing an actual JSONL→Postgres migration. Per the audit in `docs/plans/plan-2026-05-10-axon-only-architecture.md`, production AxonStore deployment requires migrating existing `.ddx/beads.jsonl` corpora into Lakebase Postgres. The real importer is unimplemented.

# ROOT CAUSE
`cli/internal/bead/migrate.go` (function previously at `*Store.MigrateToAxon`, now folded into `*storeMigrator` per MIG bead) writes JSONL into a sidecar directory. The function does not connect to Postgres or invoke `AxonStore.Apply`/`AxonStore.Create`. Production deployment cannot use it to populate Lakebase.

# PROPOSED FIX

1. Implement a real importer in `cli/internal/bead/internal/storage/import_postgres.go` (or equivalent post-BL location). The importer:
   - Reads `.ddx/beads.jsonl` (and `.ddx/beads-archive.jsonl` if present) — uses existing JSONL parser.
   - For each bead, calls `axonStore.Create(ctx, &b)` to insert into Postgres. Idempotent on `ErrConflict` (skip + log).
   - For each bead, appends events via `axonStore.Apply(ctx, id, AppendEventOp{...})` per TD-027 §13 vocabulary (events come from `Extra["events"]` on the JSONL bead or from sidecar `.ddx/attachments/<bead>/events.jsonl`).
   - Tracks progress, supports `--dry-run` (counts without writes), `--limit N` (batched migration).
   - Verifies post-migration: read back beads from Postgres, assert field-for-field equality with source JSONL (sample-based for large corpora).

2. Expose via `Migrator` interface (added in MIG): rename/replace `MigrateToAxon` with the real implementation; same method name, different (correct) behavior.

3. Update `cli/cmd/bead_migrate.go` to support the importer with appropriate flags:
   - `ddx bead migrate --to-axon --dry-run`
   - `ddx bead migrate --to-axon --apply [--limit N]`
   - `ddx bead migrate --to-axon --verify` (read-back + checksum)

4. Remove the deprecated JSONL-writing behavior. `ErrDeprecated` return from MIG bead is replaced by the real implementation.

# NON-SCOPE
- Schema-versioning migration ladder (separate concern; deferred until first v1→v2 breaking change).
- Real-wire integration test against actual Postgres — separate WIRE bead.
- Continuous sync between JSONL and Postg

The current Axon migration path still rewrites the JSONL corpus into `.ddx/axon/` instead of inserting rows into Postgres. `cli/internal/bead/migrate.go:623-680` reads active + archive JSONL and calls `writeAxonSnapshotFiles`, while `cli/internal/bead/migrator.go:54-57` still returns `ErrDeprecated` from `MigrateToAxon`. There is no importer path that exercises `axonStore.Create` / `axonStore.Apply`.

# ROOT CAUSE
`migrateToAxon` is a snapshot copier, not a data importer. It deduplicates in-memory beads and serializes them back to axon JSONL files, which cannot populate Lakebase Postgres or prove row-level write semantics.

# PROPOSED FIX
- Add `cli/internal/bead/internal/storage/import_postgres.go` or equivalent with an importer that reads `.ddx/beads.jsonl` and `.ddx/beads-archive.jsonl`.
- Use `axonStore.Create(ctx, &b)` for bead insertion and `axonStore.Apply(ctx, id, AppendEventOp{...})` for event appends.
- Treat conflict errors as idempotent skips, not failures.
- Preserve event ordering by `created_at` when appending imported events.
- Add tests that prove the write path uses the Postgres-style API and is safe to re-run.

# NON-SCOPE
- Dry-run accounting, verification read-back, and attachment upload behavior.
- CLI flag parsing and command help changes.

The bead's <description> and <acceptance> are the contract: every AC must be provably satisfied by a specific code, test, or file after your commit.

## Step 0: size check

Too big if any holds:

- More than ~6 ACs spanning unrelated subsystems.
- AC mixes design, implementation, integration tests, and docs.
- Description names multiple feature-sized pieces.
- More than ~500 lines across ~5+ files in unrelated packages.
- If the bead description exceeds 8000 bytes, use a split-first pass.
- Auto-decomposition is capped at depth 2: root beads may split once, children once more; reject third-level splits with an explanation.

If too big, decompose:

1. `ddx bead create` for each child (copy parent's labels and spec-id).
2. `ddx bead dep add` only for legitimate child-to-child or sibling/replacement edges; never the parent.
3. Use `parent=<parent-id>` metadata and wire `parent -> child`.
4. Write `no_changes_rationale.txt` under the bead metadata `bundle` path with child IDs, then stop.

Decomposition alone is success. Don't mix it with implementation.

## How to work

- Read first. If the bead names files, specs, or prior beads, read them before editing — do not guess.
- Cross-reference each AC to concrete evidence (test, file, function) before committing. If you cannot point at it, it is not done.
- Run the project's test and lint commands before committing. **Do not commit red code** — fix failures first.
- Run git/index mutations sequentially; don't parallelize `git add`, `git commit`, or staging/commit commands.
- Stage with `git add <specific-paths>`; never `git add -A` (the worktree may have unrelated WIP).
- If `lefthook run pre-commit` depends on staged files, rerun it after staging the exact commit set. A `no-staged-files` run is not acceptance evidence.
- Commit exactly once when green; subject ends with `[<bead-id>]`. Stop after the commit.
- Do not modify files outside the bead's scope.
- Current-bead lifecycle is orchestrator-owned. Do not run `ddx bead update <bead-id> --claim`, `ddx bead update <bead-id> --status <status>`, `ddx bead update <bead-id> --unclaim`, or `ddx bead close <bead-id>`. Step 0 allows `ddx bead create`, `ddx bead dep add` for child-to-child or sibling/replacement edges, and `ddx bead update <parent-id> --notes 'decomposed into <child-ids>'`.
- If you cannot finish, write `no_changes_rationale.txt` under the bead metadata `bundle` path before exiting. No commit or rationale ⇒ DDx records `no_evidence_produced`.

## no_changes contract

The `no_changes_rationale.txt` file under the bead metadata `bundle` path must contain one of:

- `verification_command: <cmd>` — repo cwd; exit 0 closes, nonzero rejects.
- `status: open` + `reason: <retryable>` — open, smart retry.
- `status: proposed` + `reason: <operator needed>` — operator lane.
- `status: blocked` + `reason: <external blocker>` — blocked lane.

No pseudo-statuses. Bare rationales and `status: needs_investigation` are rejected.

For orchestrator decomposition, add `orchestrator_action: decompose` alongside `status: open`.

## Reports

Reports go under the bead metadata `bundle` path in `.ddx/executions/`. **Never write reports to `/tmp` or outside the repo**. Use a named in-repo path when given; otherwise write `<short-name>.md` there. Commit it with the code.

## Mode and overrides

DDX_MODE=bead_execution: edit code/docs for bead AC. Only broad queue-steward default is overridden; tracker, merge-policy, verification, safety stay active. Bead description/AC override CLAUDE.md, AGENTS.md, and defaults.

## Review gate

- The review is a gate, not an escape hatch — meet every AC in this pass.
- Address every BLOCKING `<review-findings>` item; do not declare `no_changes` with blocking findings open.

## Constraints

- Work only inside this execution worktree.
- Keep `.ddx/executions/` intact — DDx uses it as execution evidence.
- **Never run `ddx init`** — the workspace is initialized.
- Do not modify files outside the bead's named scope.
- Do not rewrite CLAUDE.md, AGENTS.md, or other instruction files unless the bead asks.

## Long-running matrix/benchmark beads

For expensive commands (benchmarks, load tests, validation > 60s per variant):

- Write a matrix plan before launching expensive commands: list required configs, output paths, completion criteria.
- Do not re-run the same long-running command unless: (1) the command fingerprint changed (args/env/config), AND (2) you document why prior output is invalid and what changed.
- If a long-running command times out or is incomplete, exit with `status: open` + retryable `reason` in `no_changes_rationale.txt`. Do not silently retry; let the orchestrator decide.

## When the work is done

After the commit succeeds and every AC is verified, stop. Return control to the orchestrator.   </instructions>   <bead id="ddx-cdd6d2df">     <title>Beads can close task_succeeded while their own named AC test gate is lef: part 2</title>     <description> PROBLEM
Parent bead ddx-956e5f63 is too broad for one execution pass. This child owns part 2 of 2 of that parent scope.

ROOT CAUSE
PROBLEM: A bead executed via `ddx try`/`ddx work` can land, close with outcome=task_succeeded, and be merged -- while the exact test command its own acceptance criteria named as a required gate is left failing on the merged result.

CONCRETE EXAMPLE (project: cayce, bead cayce-8acffc83, "Microsite: render Tier 1 real-agent-conversation demos from IP-008 captures"): the bead's AC required `cd website && npm run test:e2e` to pass. It closed task_succeeded. Independently re-running that exact command on the merged commit showed 4 real failures -- two Playwright specs asserting content on two new pages the bead's own diff added references to (website/e2e/sections.spec.ts, website/e2e/visual-snapshots.spec.ts), because the two referenced pages (website/content/demos/basic-single-send.md, website/content/demos/multi-step-sequence.md) were never actually generated and committed. The bead built the renderer script (website/scripts/render-flow-demos.py) and its own unit tests using isolated fixtures (which passed), and separately updated a landing page to link the two pages that don't exist, but the actual generate-and-commit step for the real deliverable never happened.

ROOT CAUSE (most likely): `ddx try` runs each attempt in an isolated worktree. The renderer needed `target/cayce-agent-flow-transcripts/*.json`, a project-gitignored build artifact that only existed in the requesting session's own long-lived main checkout (from manual verification runs earlier in that session), not in the fresh isolated worktree the attempt actually ran in. The executing agent had no real input to render its actual deliverable against, so it could only validate via isolated-fixture unit tests -- and apparently never treated "the exact AC-named integration test command actually passes against my own worktree's real build" as a precondition for declaring success, or didn't notice its own newly-added Playwright specs were failing before finishing.

IMPACT: an operator (or a supervising agent) who trusts a closed/task_succeeded status without independently re-running the bead's own AC commands will ship broken links / failing test suites to production. This defeats a core value proposition of AC-gated execution.

PROPOSED FIX (not investigated in depth -- filed by an external observer, not a ddx maintainer):
- Before a `ddx try` attempt reports success, if the bead's acceptance criteria
...[truncated]

PROPOSED FIX
Implement only this child acceptance slice:
- Some AC-command-verification gate (either automatic exact-command re-run, or a required explicit executing-agent acknowledgment before declaring success) exists so a bead cannot close task_succeeded while its own literal AC command is failing.
- A regression test reproduces the cayce-8acffc83 shape: an attempt whose own newly-added test file asserts something the attempt's own diff didn't actually deliver.

NON-SCOPE
Do not implement parent acceptance criteria assigned to sibling child beads. Preserve every parent non-scope constraint unless this child explicitly narrows it.     </description>     <acceptance> 1. data.text: `/home` seems to be the failing filesystem for Go cache writes, even though the raw-space numbers look fine. I’m moving the cache to the `/Users` volume, which has enough headroom, so the build can stop tripping over that mount.
